coredata.dc:title: Morphological insights into uncompetitive fluorescence of coal-based carbon dots and strategies for improvement
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Mingjin
Wang, Channa
Liu, Xue
Ding, Xiangdong
Xiang, Changsheng
coredata.dc:description: The findings of the research indicate that coal-derived carbon dots (CDs), produced through top-down methods, exist as a blend of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). Within this mixture, numerous individual particles are dispersed, each containing multiple sp2-carbon domains, suggesting the presence of multiple fluorescent centers of varying sizes. These fluorescent centers scatter photon energy, leading to a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM >80 nm). To tackle this issue, the study significantly improves the fluorescence efficiency of coal-based CDs by employing a nitrogen-doping approach. Additionally, these nitrogen-doped CDs are combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to formulate secure inks exhibiting solid-state fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) properties, showcasing their potential for anti-counterfeiting applications in safeguarding important documents and artworks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal-based carbon dots
Carbon dots morphologies
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs-PVA security inks and films
Size-separated and surface state modulation of coal-based CDs
Performance enhancement and anti-counterfeiting applications of coal-based CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and optical characterization results of a-CDs-g and a-CDs-y. (a) TEM image of a-CDs-g and (b) corresponding particle size distribution. (c) Three-dimensional (3D) AFM image of a-CDs-g. (d) Height statistics of a-CDs-g. (e) TEM image of a-CDs-y and (f) corresponding particle size distribution. (g) 3D AFM image of a-CDs-y. (h) Height statistics of a-CDs-y. (i) The fluorescence spectra of the a-CDs-g and reduced a-CDs-g, excited at 365 nm. The inset shows a fluorescence image of the CDs, left：reduced a-CDs-g, right: a-CDs-g. (j) The fluorescence spectra of the a-CDs-y and reduced a-CDs-y, excited at 365 nm. The inset shows a fluorescence image of the CDs, left：reduced a-CDs-y, right: a-CDs-y. (k) The C 1s XPS spectrum of a-CDs-g. (l) The C 1s XPS spectrum of reduced a-CDs-g.
a-CDs derivation process. (a) TEM and (d) HR-TEM images of the single-unit a-CDs. (b) TEM and (e) HR-TEM images of the double-units a-CDs. (e) TEM and (f) HR-TEM images of the triple-units a-CDs.

Types of coal-based CDs. (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis and types of coal-based CDs. (b) HR-TEM image of the GQDs, the inset is the 2D FFT image that shows the crystalline hexagonal structure of GQDs. (c) HR-TEM image of the monocrystalline CQDs. (d) HR-TEM image of the polycrystalline CNDs. (e) HR-TEM image of the graphite phase CNDs. (f) and (g) HR-TEM images of the non-crystalline CNDs.

Optimized fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of coal-based CDs. (a) The PL spectra of the N-a-CDs-B and N-a-CDs-G excited at 365 nm. The concentration of the CDs solutions was 0.01 mg mL- 1 in water and the pH was ∼7. (b) PL and RTP emission spectra of N-a-CDs-B-PVA film under different wavelengths. (c) PL and RTP emission spectra of N-a-CDs-G-PVA film under different wavelengths. (d) RTP decay spectra of N-a-CDs-B-PVA film and N-a-CDs-G-PVA film. (e) PL decay spectra of N-a-CDs-B-PVA film and N-a-CDs-G-PVA film. (f) The UV-vis absorption spectra of N-a-CDs-B-PVA film, N-a-CDs-B-PVA aqueous solution, and N-a-CDs-B in water. (g) Fluorescence and phosphorescence photographs of N-a-CDs-B-PVA films and N-a-CDs-G-PVA films excited at 365 nm.
Anti-counterfeiting demonstration of the RTP CDs. (a) N-a-CDs-B-PVA and N-a-CDs-G-PVA security inks for instant marking. (b) N-a-CDs-B-PVA and N-a-CDs-G-PVA security inks for paintings and artwork.

head.ce:title: Morphological insights into uncompetitive fluorescence of coal-based carbon dots and strategies for improvement

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Mechanical Behavior of Materials, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Mechanical Behavior of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Mechanical Behavior of Materials, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The findings of the research indicate that coal-derived carbon dots (CDs), produced through top-down methods, exist as a blend of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). Within this mixture, numerous individual particles are dispersed, each containing multiple sp2-carbon domains, suggesting the presence of multiple fluorescent centers of varying sizes. These fluorescent centers scatter photon energy, leading to a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM >80 nm). To tackle this issue, the study significantly improves the fluorescence efficiency of coal-based CDs by employing a nitrogen-doping approach. Additionally, these nitrogen-doped CDs are combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to formulate secure inks exhibiting solid-state fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) properties, showcasing their potential for anti-counterfeiting applications in safeguarding important documents and artworks.

ce:section.ce:para: Coal has served as a significant source of energy for human civilization for centuries, being utilized for power generation, heating, and metallurgy, among other purposes [1]. However, with the increasing implementation of environmental policies and the development of new energy sources, the traditional coal industry has encountered challenges [2]. To ensure efficient resource utilization, coal must be utilized in a cleaner manner. Therefore, scientists are dedicated to upgrading coal into cleaner and higher value-added carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon dots (CDs) [3]. CDs are carbon nanoparticles that are smaller than 10 nm in size [4]. They possess tunable photoluminescence emissions, good biocompatibility, unique photoelectron transfer properties, and abundant surface functional groups, making them promising for a wide range of applications including tracers, anticounterfeiting measures, bioimaging systems, illumination devices, displays, photocatalysis, ion detection, lithium-ion batteries, supercapacitors, composite additives, and more [5]. CDs have been shown to be scalable carbon nanomaterial that can be derived from coal, thus offering promise as a contributor to clean coal conversion efforts [6].

The fluorescence emission of CDs can be modulated by controlling the surface functional groups. For example, as the degree of oxidation on the CD surface increases, the corresponding fluorescence emission gradually redshifts. This is attributed to the increase in oxygen-containing functional groups such as –OH and –COOH, leading to more surface defects [7]. Additionally, heteroatom doping is considered a conventional method for effectively tuning the fluorescence emission of CDs. For instance, doping with nitrogen (N) atoms, especially increasing the content of graphitic N in the carbon crystallite domains, can create defect states between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) band gaps, resulting in fluorescence redshift [8]. However, these methods mostly adjust the emission wavelength and quantum yield (QY) of CDs, with little discussion on their impact on full width at half maximum (FWHM). CDs with narrow emission bandwidths (FWHM <60 nm) possess advantages such as pure fluorescence color and resistance to background fluorescence interference [9,10]. Currently, most reported CDs with narrow bandwidth emission are synthesized via bottom-up approaches [11]; coal-based CDs typically exhibit an uncompetitive broad FWHM (>80 nm) [12]. The photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of CDs remains a subject of debate among researchers, with four types of PL mechanisms mostly accepted to explain the tunable emission of CDs, including the carbon core state (quantum confinement effect of conjugated π-domains), surface state (functional groups or surface defects), molecular state and crosslink-enhanced emission effect [13]. Among them, the synergistic effect of the carbon core state and surface state is considered the main PL mechanism of CDs prepared via the top-down approaches [14]. The top-down method for the preparation of coal-based CDs results in an ambiguous product structure due to the complexity of the raw materials, hindering further mechanism research and performance improvement of coal-based CDs. Furthermore, there is limited literature on strategies for enhancing the fluorescence performance of coal-based CDs, and the relationship between the FWHM and the structure of coal-based CDs has been seldom addressed. Hence, it is essential to explore the origins of the broad-bandwidth emission in coal-based CDs to facilitate their advancement.

To investigate the morphological characteristics of coal-based CDs prepared via a top-down approach, this work focused on utilizing two representative coals, anthracite with higher degree of graphitization, and lignite with lower degree of graphitization, to produce coal-based CDs. The investigation into the fluorescence properties of these CDs focused on both carbon core and surface state mechanisms. Extensive high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) observation revealed that the coal-based CDs are mixtures with many dispersed particles exhibiting multiple sp2-carbon domains, indicating multiple fluorescent centers of different sizes. This morphology contributes to their suboptimal fluorescence performance. Hence, this work employs N doping to significantly enhance the fluorescence performance of coal-based CDs. These N-doped coal-based CDs, when combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution, can be used to produce security inks with solid-state fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) properties. Compared to CDs prepared by bottom-up methods, the low cost and ease of preparation of coal-based CDs make them promising in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

Anthracite and lignite were obtained from Taixi Coal Mine and Zhundong Coal Mine in China, respectively. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95-98%), nitric acid (HNO3, 65-68%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) and ammonia monohydrate (NH3·H2O, 25-28%) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. PVA (MW: 67000), ethylenediamine (EDA), m-phenylenediamine (MPD) were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals. All ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes were purchased from the Pall Co., Ltd., USA. All reagents were used as received without any further purification. All aqueous solutions used throughout the experiment were prepared with deionized water.

The morphology and microstructure of the CDs were determined by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200) at 200 kV. CDs size distribution was measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Multimode8 SPM) operating at the tapping mode. Specifically, the CDs dispersion was diluted first, then spincoated on the mica slip disk with a diameter of 1 cm. AFM probe was used to scan an area of 1 μm × 1 μm on mica disk, and the size distributions of CDs were observed. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo Escalab Xi+ spectrometer. The UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1780 UV-vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra and phosphorescence spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-4700 spectrophotometer. The FL3C-111 TCSPC instrument system (HORIBA, Japan) was used to measure the time-resolved fluorescence decay curve under 365 nm excitation. The sample’s absolute QY was measured by the OmniFluo990 LSP instrument (Zolix, China) and an integrating sphere. The CDs powder was dissolved in deionized water with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Raman scattering spectra were obtained using Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer with 325 nm laser. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (γ = 0.15418 nm) in the range of 10-80° at room temperature. The Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) experiment was carried out on a Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer and used a wavenumber range of 400-4000 cm- 1. The samples were prepared as pellets together with Potassium bromide (KBr).

Anthracite sample (5 g) was dispersed in a mixed solvent of 180 mL H2SO4 and 60 mL HNO3. The solution was sonicated for 2 h and then heated at 120 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally, and then poured into a beaker containing 2 L of ice. NaOH was added to the solution while stirring until the solution reached a pH of ∼7. The resulting solution was subjected to filtration using a 200 nm membrane followed by purification and concentration using cross-flow ultrafiltration system equipped with a 500 Da membrane. The 500 Da concentrates were subjected to gradient centrifugation for 30 min at 4000 rpm using ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes with molecular weight cutoffs of 30 kD, 10 kD, 3 kD, and 1 kD, respectively. Finally, the obtained solution was subjected to vacuum freeze-drying, yielding a-CDs-g (500 Da-1 kD) and a-CDs-y (3 kD-10 kD).

Lignite sample (5 g) was poured into a 1 L beaker containing 300 mL of H2O2. The beaker was placed in a sonicator at room temperature and sonicated at a frequency of 40 kHz for 6 h. After sonication, NH3·H2O was slowly added dropwise into the beaker to adjust the solution pH ∼7. The resulting solution was subjected to filtration using a 200 nm membrane followed by purification and concentration using cross-flow ultrafiltration system equipped with a 500 Da membrane. The 500 Da concentrates were subjected to gradient centrifugation for 30 min at 4000 rpm using ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes with molecular weight cutoffs of 30 kD, 10 kD, 3 kD, and 1 kD, respectively. Finally, the obtained solution was subjected to vacuum freeze-drying, yielding l-CDs-c (1 kD-3 kD) and l-CDs-g (3 kD-10 kD).

50 mL of 1 mg mL- 1 aqueous solution of a-CDs-g and a-CDs-y were separately mixed with 100 mg of NaBH4 and stirred at room temperature for 6 h. After that, the solutions were subjected to cross-flow ultrafiltration (500 Da) and vacuum freeze-drying as described above. The resulting products were reduced a-CDs-g and reduced a-CDs-y.

The a-CDs-g aqueous solution (20 mL, 2 mg mL- 1) was mixed with 10 mg EDA, 10 mg MPD in 10 mL of deionized water, respectively. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After that, the solutions were subjected to cross-flow ultrafiltration (500 Da) and vacuum freeze-drying as described above. The resulting products were N-a-CDs-B and N-a-CDs-G.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-PVA security inks and films
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g of PVA powder was slowly added to 50 mL of water, and the solution was stirred continuously for 24 h at room temperature to ensure complete dissolution of PVA. After that, at room temperature, 5 mg of N-a-CDs-B and 5 mg of N-a-CDs-G were added respectively, and stirring was continued for 12 h. Finally, N-a-CDs-B-PVA and N-a-CDs-G-PVA security inks were obtained. Ink pens were created by utilizing a syringe to inject a portion of the solution into a clean, empty refill. The ink was poured out, and it was allowed to dry naturally to obtain N-a-CDs-B-PVA film and N-a-CDs-G-PVA film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size-separated and surface state modulation of coal-based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a-CDs were prepared using the one-step wet-chemistry route [15], while l-CDs were prepared using a wet-chemical ultrasonic irradiated technique [16,17]. Both types of the as-synthesized CDs obtained were rigorously purified and size-separated through cross-flow ultrafiltration and ultrafiltration centrifugation, yielding green-fluorescent a-CDs (a-CDs-g), yellow-fluorescent a-CDs (a-CDs-y), cyan-fluorescent l-CDs (l-CDs-c), and green-fluorescent l-CDs (l-CDs-g). TEM and AFM were employed to characterize the sizes of a-CDs-g and a-CDs-y, as shown in Fig. 1 , which confirmed the successful implementation of the size separation procedure. Fig. 1a and b showed that a-CDs-g were spherical and well dispersed. The average particle size was 2.07 ± 0.02 nm. From the results of AFM measurements, the particle height was 1.08 ± 0.01 nm (Fig. 1c and d). The experimental results show that a-CDs-g have a small particle size and good uniformity. Similarly, a-CDs-y also exhibit good uniformity, with an average particle size larger than that of a-CDs-g, at 3.36 ± 0.03 nm, as shown in Fig. 1e and f. AFM characterization results indicated that the particle height of a-CDs-y was 1.55 ± 0.01 nm (Fig. 1g and h). TEM was employed to characterize the sizes of l-CDs-c and l-CDs-g, as shown in Fig. S1, like a-CDs, the two types of l-CDs also exhibit size differences, with average particle sizes of 2.49 ± 0.04 nm (l-CDs-c) and 3.14 ± 0.1 nm (l-CDs-g). This is consistent with previous reports that CDs have size-dependent fluorescence emission (quantum confinement effect) and that the band gap can be adjusted by controlling the CDs size to achieve multicolor fluorescence emission [7,18–20]. Basic XRD and Raman spectra characterizations of these products can be found in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3. XRD characterization revealed the amorphous nature of both types of a-CDs, while Raman spectra indicated sp2-and sp3-hybrid structures with ID/IG values of 0.41 (a-CDs-g) and 0.37 (a-CDs-y), respectively (Fig. S2). Due to the lower graphitization level of lignite, both types of l-CDs exhibit poorer crystallinity and higher amorphous carbon content compared to a-CDs (Fig. S3).

However, the rigorous size separation procedure did not effectively reduce the FWHM. The fluorescence spectra of a-CDs and l-CDs before and after size separation are shown in Fig. S4. Before size separation, the QY of a-CDs and l-CDs were 0.51 % and 0.63 %, respectively; The FWHM of a-CDs was 144 nm, while l-CDs had a FWHM of 178 nm. After size separation, the QY of the products were as follows: 0.53% (a-CDs-g), 0.31% (a-CDs-y), 1.62% (l-CDs-c), and 0.71% (l-CDs-g); The FWHM of the products were: 147 nm (a-CDs-g), 141 nm (a-CDs-y), 176 nm (l-CDs-c), and 171 nm (l-CDs-g). Hence, the particle size distribution of coal-based CDs is not the fundamental cause of the broad FWHM. NaBH4 is a common reducing agent capable of reducing oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as carbonyl, amide, and epoxy groups, to hydroxyl groups under ambient conditions [21,22]. In order to explore the impact of surface state on the fluorescence performance of coal-based CDs, the a-CDs-g and a-CDs-y were subjected to reduction using NaBH4, resulting in the production of reduced a-CDs-g and reduced a-CDs-y, respectively [23]. Fig. 1i and j illustrate the fluorescence spectra of the two a-CDs before and after the reduction treatment in neutral aqueous media, respectively. Both types of a-CDs exhibited reduced FWHM, blue-shifted fluorescence, and increased QY after reduction treatment with NaBH4. At the same concentration (0.01 mg mL- 1), the fluorescence intensity of reduced a-CDs-g is 6.2 times higher than that of a-CDs-g, the absolute QY increased from 0.53% (a-CDs-g), 0.31% (a-CDs-y) to 1.43% (reduced a-CDs-g) and 0.65% (reduced a-CDs-y), the FWHM is reduced by 24 nm, and the maximum emission peak is blue-shifted from 515 nm to 471 nm, indicating a significant color change from green to blue in fluorescence.

XPS is employed to elucidate the effect of NaBH4 on the removal of surface functional groups from CDs. As shown in Fig. S5a, the surfaces of a-CDs-g and reduced a-CDs-g contain only C, O, and N elements, with negligible changes in atomic percentages before and after the reduction treatment. The C 1s spectrum of a-CDs-g can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8 eV (74.49%), 286.3 eV (3.35%), and 288.3 eV (21.96%), corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-OH, and C=O bonds (Fig. 1k) [24]. The C 1s spectrum of reduced a-CDs-g also shows three types of carbon bonds (C-C/C=C, C-OH, and C=O) at 284.8 eV (75.42%), 286.4 eV (20.34%), and 288.6 eV (4.24%) (Fig. 1l) [25]. Due to the reduction effect of NaBH4, carbonyl and epoxy groups on the surface of the CDs are reduced to hydroxyl groups, resulting in a significant increase in the C-OH content on the surface of reduced a-CDs-g. Modulating the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of coal-based CDs will aid in exploring the impact of surface state mechanisms on their fluorescence properties. It should be noted that the N atom content on the surfaces of a-CDs-g and reduced a-CDs-g is very low, at 2.01% and 1.74%, respectively. As shown in Figs. S5b and S5c, the N 1s spectra of both samples exhibit nearly identical peak positions at 399.5 eV, 405.6 eV, and 406.9 eV, which can be attributed to pyrrolic N, nitro N, and -CONH2 (N-containing dangling bonds or physisorbed species), respectively [26–28]. More details can be found in Table S1. The N functional groups likely originate from coal and/or HNO3 oxidation, which induces the doping of N into the CDs. After the reduction treatment, the percentage of N-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs shows no significant change. As shown in Fig. S6a, in the UV-vis spectra, a-CDs-g exhibited a broad absorption band around 280 nm, which can be attributed to n-π* transitions of the C=O [29]. In addition, the broad absorption band around 325 nm may be attributed to the N-doping effect. The C=O absorption peak of reduced a-CDs-g at ∼280 nm is almost invisible, while the absorption peak at ∼220 nm is attributed to the π-π* transitions of C=C bonds [30]. Furthermore, the FT-IR characterization results also confirmed the effect of NaBH4 on the removal of oxygen-containing species on the surface of CDs (Fig. S6b). The a-CDs-g shows a C=O (carbonyl/carboxyl) stretching peak at 1720 cm- 1 and C-O (epoxy) stretching peak at 1257 cm- 1, which become weak or even disappear completely after the reduction treatment [31]. In the oxidation step for the coal-based CDs, the surfaces and edges of the carbonized crystallites in coal are partially oxidized to form oxygen-containing functionalities. The surface and edge defects associated with the CO-related localized electronic states at the oxidation sites might be responsible for the long-wavelength emissions in the CDs [32]. Reduction treatment with NaBH4 removed oxygen-containing species on the surface of the CDs, restoring the π network structure of reduced a-CDs-g [33]. This suppression of long-wavelength emission and enhancement of short-wavelength emission resulted in a significant increase in fluorescence intensity accompanied by a noticeable blue-shift and decreased FWHM [34]. However, after the reduction treatment, the FWHM of coal-based CDs remained greater than 100 nm (the FWHM of reduced a-CDs-g and reduced a-CDs-y were 123 nm and 139 nm, respectively). Hence, the surface state (oxygen-containing functional groups) may not be the key cause of the broad FWHM of coal-based CDs.

To further elucidate the origins of the wide FWHM of CDs, HR-TEM was conducted to observe the lattice structure of a-CDs. Fig. 2 a-c shows the TEM images of the a-CDs with single, double and triple units within one particle. For CDs with double and triple units, these units are presumably connected by amorphous carbon and bridging bonds such as benzylic systems, C-O-C and C(O)O [35,36]. Fig. 2d shows the HR-TEM image of the single-unit a-CDs, revealing the existence of three crystalline orientations, indicating their polycrystalline nature. Fig. 2e shows the HR-TEM image of the double-units a-CDs, which has two crystalline orientations of sp2-carbon domains. The two units seems to be connected by amorphous carbon, which could be broken upon further oxidation. The triple-units a-CDs also exhibit similar features as double-units CDs, as shown in Fig. 2f. Additional TEM images of the a-CDs with similar morphology features are available in Fig. S7. It is noteworthy that, apart from CDs derived from anthracite, CDs prepared from lignite also exhibit a similar morphology, as shown in Fig. S8. The above observations indicate the existence of intermediate states during the synthesis process of coal-based CDs, and the CDs gradually dissociate from the coal matrix as the degree of oxidation cleavage deepens. The sp2-carbon domains exhibit non-uniformity in size, crystallinity, and are embedded in a disordered manner within the intrinsic 3D matrix of coal. Even with harsh oxidation methods, it is almost impossible to completely detach all the CDs from the coal matrix due to the structural complexity of the bridging bonds and their differences in dissociation energy.

Hence, the structural consistency of coal-based CDs cannot be ensured. Based on the comprehensive HR-TEM observation of coal-based CDs produced through the top-down method, they can be classified into three types: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) [37], as shown in Fig. 3 a. Fig. 3b shows a typical graphene lattice and the two-dimensional (2D) FFT image shows the crystalline hexagonal structure (inset), thus indicating that it can be classified as GQDs [38]. In fact, the GQDs observed by TEM are very rare, and most coal-based CDs belong to CQDs and CNDs. CDs that possess the features of monodispersity, approximate sphericity, single crystal plane orientation, and interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm are often referred to as CQDs (Fig. 3c) [39]. The third category of coal-based CDs is CNDs, which share similar sizes with GQDs and CQDs but lack distinct quantum confinement effects, presenting three distinct structures. Polycrystalline CDs with an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm, as shown in Fig. 3d, can be classified as CNDs. As shown in Fig. 3e, the CDs exhibit a crystal plane spacing of 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite [40]. This indicates that they are different from the single-layer or few-layer carbon atomic structure of GQDs and CQDs but belong to the CNDs of graphite phase. The last type of CNDs is amorphous, as shown in Fig. 3f and g, which may originate from the amorphous carbon in coal. TEM observations demonstrate that coal-based CDs produced through the top-down method consist of a combination of GQDs, CQDs, and CNDs existing simultaneously. The similarity in size of these CDs presents a challenge for their separation and purification. Although monodisperse CDs can be obtained through a rigorous size separation procedure (Fig. 1), their structure remains disordered. Additional HR-TEM images of a-CDs-g and a-CDs-y are shown in Fig. S9. The various forms of existence may confuse their functions and make theoretical studies on coal-based CDs difficult. The basic unit of coal-based CDs fluorescence emission is not the CDs particles, but the sp2-carbon domains in its carbon core [41]. Monodisperse CDs particles can have single crystal, polycrystalline, or twinned structures, in which there may be a series of sp2-carbon domains of different sizes, leading to polycrystalline region emissions in each CDs particle [42]. Hence, the complex emission from polycrystalline regions may be the root cause for the broad FWHM of coal-based CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance enhancement and anti-counterfeiting applications of coal-based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence performance of pristine coal-based CDs is non-competitive; hence, a series of post-treatment methods must be employed to enhance their fluorescence performance for certain optical applications [43]. Among these methods, heteroatom doping is a simple and effective approach [44,45]. In this work, using EDA and MPD as N sources, N-doped blue-fluorescent coal-based CDs (N-a-CDs-B) and N-doped green-fluorescent coal-based CDs (N-a-CDs-G) with significantly improved fluorescence performance were successfully prepared via a hydrothermal reaction. Composite films with solid-state fluorescence and RTP were fabricated by blending these CDs with a PVA solution. Relevant characterization data are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a illustrates the fluorescence spectra of N-a-CDs-B and N-a-CDs-G aqueous solutions when excited at a wavelength of 365 nm. For N-a-CDs-B, the FWHM is 92 nm with an absolute QY is 31%, and an emission peak at 450 nm. N-a-CDs-G, on the other hand, exhibit an FWHM of 103 nm, with an absolute QY of 23%, and an emission peak at 516 nm. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, the prepared N-a-CDs-B-PVA film and N-a-CDs-G-PVA film exhibit attractive solid-state fluorescence and RTP properties. Under multiple excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence emission peak of N-a-CDs-B-PVA film is at ∼425 nm, and the phosphorescence emission peak is at ∼500 nm. Both emission peaks remain almost constant, indicating excitation-independent emission. Interestingly, the solid-state fluorescence of N-a-CDs-B-PVA film is blue-shifted by 25 nm compared to the fluorescence in the N-a-CDs-B aqueous solution, and the FWHM is reduced to 44 nm. This blue shift may be attributed to surface state emission, as CDs tend to aggregate when freely moving in solution, thereby exhibiting longer wavelength fluorescence. However, due to the constraining effect of hydrogen bonding formed between the PVA matrix and CDs, the motion of CDs is restricted, leading to uniform dispersion and enhanced surface state emission, resulting in fluorescence blue shift [46]. Furthermore, the stable network structure formed by hydrogen bonding between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs surface and the hydroxyl groups of PVA restrains non-radiative relaxation, leading to further enhancement of the CDs fluorescence intensity [47]. Simultaneously, it promotes intersystem crossing (ISC), resulting in RTP. The FWHM of RTP in N-a-CDs-B-PVA film is 77 nm. Upon calculation, the energy gap (ΔE ST) between the singlet and triplet states in N-a-CDs-B-PVA film is determined to be 0.41 eV [48]. In contrast to N-a-CDs-B-PVA film, when excited at a wavelength of 365 nm, N-a-CDs-G-PVA film exhibits a fluorescence emission peak at around 490 nm with a FWHM of 90 nm. The RTP emission peak is observed at 530 nm, with an increased FWHM of 104 nm. Calculations indicate that the ΔE ST between the singlet and triplet states in N-a-CDs-G-PVA film is 0.21 eV. Generally, if ΔE ST between the triplet and singlet states is ≤ 0.2 eV, due to thermal effects, the triplet excitons can undergo reverse intersystem crossing (RISC), resulting in delayed fluorescence exhibited by the material [49]. The schematic diagram of the fluorescence, delayed fluorescence, and phosphorescence emission processes can be found in Fig. S10. Due to the extremely close proximity of the ΔE ST value of N-a-CDs-G-PVA film to the threshold (0.2 eV), it exhibits characteristics of both delayed fluorescence and RTP, resulting in broadening of the FWHM. Time-resolved luminescence decay curves for both N-a-CDs-B-PVA film and N-a-CDs-G-PVA film in terms of fluorescence and phosphorescence were also measured. The average phosphorescence lifetimes for N-a-CDs-B-PVA film and N-a-CDs-G-PVA film were 918.26 ms and 279.28 ms, as shown in Fig. 4d, while their average fluorescence lifetimes were 8.63 ns and 3.98 ns, as presented in Fig. 4e. As demonstrated in Fig. 4f, the source of RTP in N-a-CDs-B-PVA film was discussed by analyzing the UV-vis absorption spectra of N-a-CDs-B-PVA film, N-a-CDs-B-PVA aqueous solution, and N-a-CDs-B aqueous solution. The UV-vis absorption peak positions of N-a-CDs-B-PVA aqueous solution and N-a-CDs-B aqueous solution are generally the same. Absorption peaks in the range of 210-280 nm are attributed to π-π* transitions of C=C and n-π* transitions of subfluorescent groups (-NH2, C-N) [50]. N-a-CDs-B-PVA film exhibits a strong absorption peak in the range of 330-400 nm, which is attributed to n-π* transitions of C=N and C=O [51]. Moreover, the absorption peak at around 220 nm from C=C is essentially absent, and the n-π* transitions of -NH2, C-N are inhibited. Hence, the RTP in N-a-CDs-B-PVA film is likely determined by the rigidity of the PVA matrix and originates from the n-π* transitions of C=N and C=O [52]. As shown in Fig. 4g, N-a-CDs-B-PVA film and N-a-CDs-G-PVA film exhibit bright and easily distinguishable fluorescence colors. After turning off the UV light (365 nm), N-a-CDs-B-PVA film can produce green phosphorescence with a lifetime of over 8 s, while N-a-CDs-G-PVA film can produce yellow phosphorescence with a lifetime of over 6 s. These films have potential to be combined and customized in shape for use in anti-counterfeiting labels.

When coupled with their solid-state fluorescence and RTP properties, these characteristics can be employed for anti-counterfeiting measures on important documents, artworks, and more. Fig. 5 a illustrates a flower, with the petals drawn using an ink pen loaded with N-a-CDs-B-PVA solution, and the stems and leaves drawn using an ink pen loaded with N-a-CDs-G-PVA solution. Under natural light, the flower’s petals are faintly visible, while the branches and leaves are nearly invisible. When exposed to UV light, the entire flower becomes visible, with the petals appearing blue and the branches and leaves appearing green. After turning off the UV light, the petals immediately change to green, while the branches and leaves turn yellow, and they are easily distinguishable to the human-eye. The phosphorescence of the branches and leaves disappears after 4 s, but the green phosphorescence of the petals remains clearly visible until 6 s. The scenario indicates that ink pens made from N-a-CDs-B-PVA and N-a-CDs-G-PVA solutions can be used for rapid writing and marking to achieve anti-counterfeiting purposes. Furthermore, these two types of security inks can also be customized into stamps for standardized anti-counterfeiting purposes. As shown in Fig. 5b-a customized seal was made using N-a-CDs-B-PVA and N-a-CDs-G-PVA solutions as inks, and it was stamped on a blank space of a traditional Chinese ink painting. Under natural light, no distinct mark is visible on the painting; however, under UV light, a seal script stamp appears. The two characters on the left are in cyan (formed by N-a-CDs-G-PVA ink), while the two characters on the right are in blue (formed by N-a-CDs-B-PVA ink). After turning off the UV lamp, a faint yellow phosphorescence of the two characters on the left is observed. After 3 s, the characters on the left become indistinguishable, while the characters on the right still exhibit a bright green phosphorescence until 5 s. Compared to CDs prepared by the bottom-up methods, coal-based CDs have the advantages of low cost and easy preparation, in addition, their fluorescence performance can be effectively enhanced by simple post-processing. As a proof-of-concept, coal-based CDs demonstrate significant potential in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, this work focuses on the morphological characteristics of coal-based CDs, offering a new perspective on their structure-fluorescence FWHM relationship. It reveals that coal-based CDs are mixtures of GQDs, CQDs, and CNDs, and outlines their potential structures. The study suggests that numerous dispersed polycrystalline CDs with multiple sp2-carbon domains of different sizes are key to the broad FWHM. These domains act as multiple fluorescent centers, emitting fluorescence at different wavelengths, leading to a broad FWHM independent of particle size. Furthermore, a strategy to enhance the fluorescence performance of coal-based CDs by N-doping is proposed. Benefiting from the significantly improved fluorescence performance, films formed by blending the product CDs with PVA solution exhibit enhanced solid-state fluorescence and bright RTP. Further investigation revealed that a stable network structure formed via hydrogen bonding between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs surface and the hydroxyl groups of PVA suppresses non-radiative relaxation, thereby further enhancing the fluorescence intensity of CDs. This stable structure also promotes ISC, resulting in the generation of RTP in the CDs-PVA composite films; additionally, the n-π* transitions of C=N and C=O groups synergistically contribute to RTP as well. Finally, the potential applications of ink pens and stamps made from CDs-PVA solutions are demonstrated in terms of rapid writing, marking, and standardized anti-counterfeiting. The ultra-low cost and ease of large-scale production of coal-based CDs provide them with great potential compared with CDs produced from bottom-up methods. This work provides a foundational platform for exploring the fluorescence mechanism of coal-based CDs and formulating strategies to improve their optical performance for potential applications.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Mingjin Du: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Channa Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Xue Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis. Xiangdong Ding: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration. Changsheng Xiang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
